data.title: Mortgage rates up marginally
data.description:
Mortgage rates rose marginally this week but remained below the 7% threshold, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgage rates averaged 6.89% as of May 29, up from 6.86% last week and down from 7.03% in the year-ago week.

15-year fixed-rate mortgage rates averaged 6.03%, up from 6.01% a week ago but down from 6.36% a year ago.

"Aspiring buyers should remember to shop around for the best mortgage rate, as they can potentially save thousands of dollars by getting multiple quotes," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

data.title: ADNOC buys 70% stake in SLB's Kuwait, Oman drilling rigs business in up to $112M deal
data.description:
SLB (NYSE:SLB) said Thursday that ADNOC Drilling agreed to acquire a 70% stake in its land drilling rigs business in Kuwait and Oman for as much as $112M, creating a joint venture the companies aim to double over the next year.

ADNOC Drilling will pay $91M for the stake, plus another $21M payout to SLB (NYSE:SLB) linked to business performance, ADNOC Drilling CFO Youssef Salem told Reuters.

The deal includes eight operational onshore rigs - six in Oman and two in Kuwait - under contract with the respective national oil companies of both countries.

The deal marks ADNOC Dilling's first drilling operations outside the United Arab Emirates, apart from a single rig in Jordan.

data.title: Cocoa prices fall back after improved West Africa weather and tariff de-escalation
data.description:
Cocoa prices have been in a downtrend over the last week, with futures on their longest losing streak in nearly three years. A tamping down of tariff concerns and improved weather in West Africa contributed to the selling pressure.

Cocoa futures in New York were 5.4% to $9,140 a ton in late morning trading to trade at their lowest level in more than three weeks. The 52-week high for the contract is $12,646.07.

The U.S. government imposed a blanket 10% tariff on most imports this year, including cocoa, as part of a broader trade policy shift. The tariffs are not uniform and vary by country of origin and cocoa product type. Companies have also been actively pushing for exemptions. The last week has seen generally positive news on the tariff front, which has worked itself into the cocoa trading pits as the worst-case tariff scenarios appear more unlikely.

Meanwhile, widespread rainfall in West Africa, where the bulk of the global cocoa supplies are sourced, has improved the outlook from traders on supply. However, a drought still covers more than a third of Ghana and the Ivory Coast, according to the African Flood and Drought Monitor.

"There's been some uncertainty about the weather and the impact on the crops but things are looking better than they were a month ago," stated Steve Wateridge, head of research at Tropical Research Services by Expana. "We are destroying so much demand and there’s a strong argument that the market at the moment is overvalued unless we get poor crops," he added.

Last week, the International Cocoa Organization issued a key report on the industry.

On the demand side, the International Cocoa Organization highlighted that Q1 2025 quarterly grinding data from major cocoa associations revealed a decline. The European Cocoa Association reported a drop of 3.7% (to 353,522 tons), the Cocoa Association of Asia reported a decline of 3.44% (to 213,898 tons), and the National Confectioners Association reported a decline of 2.45% (to 110,278 tons). 

"Although quarterly grindings were negative, they were better than analysts’ expectations. The grinding data signaled some demand resilience and boosted prices. With uncertainty concerning the mid-crop lingering, there are five months remaining until the end of the season. The next five months will be interesting as progress in market fundamentals will provide a clearer view of the cocoa market outlook."

Some of the companies that have mentioned the impact of high cocoa prices during earnings conference calls include Mondelez International (MDLZ), Lindt & Spruengli AG (OTCPK:LDSVF), Olam International (OTCPK:OLGPF), Nestlé S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY), Conagra Brands (CAG), Simply Good Foods (SMPL), PepsiCo (PEP), Chocoladefabriken Lindt & Sprüngli AG (OTCPK:LDSVF), 1-800-Flowers.com (FLWS), Barry Callebaut AG (OTCPK:BYCBF), J.M. Smucker Company (SJM), Rogers Sugar (OTCPK:RSGUF), Rocky Mountain Chocolate Factory (RMCF), Costco (COST), Tootsie Roll Industries (TR), Rogers Sugar Inc. (OTCPK:RSGUF), Associated British Foods plc (OTCPK:ASBFY), and J&J Snack Foods (JJSF).

data.title: Omada Health aims to raise upto $158M in IPO
data.description:
• Omada Health (OMDA) said on Thursday that it would sell 7.9M shares between the price of $18 to $20 per share, aiming to raise about $158M if priced at the upper limit, in its initial public offering.
  • The digital health startup has applied to list its common stock on the Nasdaq under the symbol 'OMDA'.
  • J.P.Morgan, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley and Barclays are among the underwriters for Omada's offering.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P rise as Nvidia boost, tariff ruling offsets weak GDP; Salesforce drags Dow
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday was largely higher, as sentiment was boosted by another blowout earnings report from Nvidia (NVDA) and a ruling by a U.S. court that struck down most of President Donald Trump's April tariffs.

Market participants also received a slightly better second reading on U.S. Q1 economic growth, though the figure remained in contraction. The GDP print helped bump up Federal Reserve rate cut expectations. 

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.2% in morning trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%. The Dow (DJI) seesawed, last -0.2%, as a post-earnings slide in Salesforce (CRM) offset the gains from Nvidia (NVDA).   

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was down 4 basis points to 4.44%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.95%.

On Wednesday, a federal trade court struck down the “reciprocal” tariffs that Trump imposed on what he called “Liberation Day,” putting an immediate stop to the collection of a sweeping set of levies that the President imposed.

“The stock market is moving higher as it's becoming clear that the administration's rhetoric on tariffs is tougher than its actual policies on tariffs. While the across the board tariff rates are higher now than they were at the beginning of the year, they are much less than the ones outlined on Liberation Day, and that is helping to push stocks higher,” said Paul Stanley, CIO at Granite Bay Wealth Management.

Meanwhile, AI darling Nvidia (NVDA) rose 4.9% after the firm reported fiscal first-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations, despite the impact of added export control curbs to China.

“Nvidia continues to defy expectations, proving it’s still powering the AI wave. Nvidia's blowout earnings reinforce the idea that innovation can still thrive even in a market full of uncertainty,” Stanley added.

Thursday's economic calendar was fairly busy, with the GDP report taking the spotlight. The Bureau of Economic Analysis revised its estimate of U.S. Q1 GDP growth to a decrease of 0.2% from a contraction of 0.3%.

"When the initial estimate of Q1 GDP became available in April, we suggested the report be considered through the lens of businesses and households trying to get ahead of the tariffs. That approach, in our view, allows many of the big pieces of the report to fall into line," Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan said. 

"What changes in today's revision is not a page-one re-write of that story, but rather one in which we now have greater details. In a nutshell, the details are not terribly encouraging," Quinlan added.      

Also, initial jobless claims jumped past consensus in the last week, while Q1 U.S. corporate profits dropped 3.6% Q/Q.

data.title: Hormel Foods narrows 2025 outlook to 2%-3% net sales growth as supply chain transformation advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hormel Foods Corporation (HRL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jim Snee, President and CEO, stated the company "achieved solid organic top-line growth and delivered second quarter results in line with our expectations," emphasizing a commitment to "profitable and predictable growth" despite a dynamic operating environment.
  • Snee highlighted the performance of the Applegate brand, noting "incredible sales growth, outpacing the total edible category, while also growing households." He also pointed to the launch of new products and ongoing innovation as key drivers.
  • The Jennie-O lean ground Turkey product was described as "a high performing and strategically important offering," with consistent consumption gains and strong category leadership.
  • Snee reported the Mexican foods portfolio saw "continued success with our legacy Herdez salsa business," and double-digit consumption growth in the refrigerated guacamole portfolio.
  • The foodservice segment "remains resilient," with branded products delivering strong volume and net sales growth. Snee mentioned innovation in pizza toppings and sous vide chicken as drivers of differentiation and operational efficiency.
  • Internationally, Snee said the business delivered "strong top line growth... driven by an impressive double-digits volume and net sales growth in exports and robust growth in China."
  • Snee provided updates on Planters and SPAM brands, noting Planters "performance exceeded our second quarter expectations" and anticipates it will be "a driving force in the second half." For SPAM, he said the brand is seeing "global momentum" and expects an "equally impressive second half."
  • Snee announced leadership changes, including Dr. Kevin Myers leading supply chain efforts, the retirement of Scott Aakre as CMO for Retail who will join the Board of Directors, and Jeff Baker set to assume retail marketing leadership in fiscal 2026.
  • "Net sales for the quarter were $2.9 billion, a 1% organic increase over last year," stated Jacinth Smiley, Executive Vice President and CFO.
  • Smiley added, "Our gross profit margin was 16.7%, reflecting anticipated higher commodity input costs... partially offset by savings from our T&M initiative."

Outlook

  • Hormel Foods narrowed its fiscal 2025 outlook, now expecting "increased net sales growth of 2% to 3%," supported by the Turkey portfolio, Planters, leading market positions, continued foodservice growth, and higher commodity markets.
  • The company expects "adjusted diluted earnings per share in the range of $1.58 to $1.68," reflecting views on consumer trends, tariffs, and investment income.
  • Smiley affirmed, "We expect each segment to deliver bottom line growth in the third quarter and second half."
  • For segment guidance: "For Retail, we expect low single-digit net sales growth. For Foodservice, we expect mid-single-digit growth in organic net sales. And for the International segment, we expect continued strong top line performance resulting in high single-digit growth."
  • Advertising investments are set to "significantly increase in the second half."

Financial Results

  • Hormel reported "net sales for the quarter were $2.9 billion, a 1% organic increase over last year."
  • "Gross profit margin was 16.7%," reflecting higher commodity input costs, partially offset by Transform & Modernize (T&M) initiative savings.
  • Operating cash flow was reported at $56 million for the quarter, with capital expenditures of $75 million.
  • "We reported diluted earnings per share of $0.33 for the second quarter and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $0.35, in line with expectations," according to Smiley.
  • The company paid its 387th consecutive quarterly dividend.

Q&A

  • Peter Galbo, Bank of America: Asked about bridging the back-half operating income ramp and T&M savings. Jim Snee responded, emphasizing the recovery in Planters, momentum in Turkey, and ongoing value-added business performance, with John Ghingo adding, "We feel confident in our second half... Our portfolio is strong in this environment."
  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: Queried about margin expansion in the second half. Smiley answered, "We do expect expansion in the second half, driven primarily by Turkey, Planters, our value-added business... and that will accelerate more even into the back half of this year."
  • Tom Palmer, Citi: Sought quantification of T&M savings and inventory build. Smiley noted 66 projects executed under T&M this quarter, including the opening of the Memphis distribution center and closure of a California facility.
  • Ben Theurer, Barclays: Asked about Planters' recovery and distribution. Ghingo explained, "The foundational work is in good shape. Our supply is corrected... We expect to see the distribution continue to ramp, the overall consumption on the brand continue to ramp."
  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research: Inquired on ground Turkey share gains. Snee indicated tightening supply but said full details are "TBD," while Ghingo highlighted Jennie-O's "consistent household penetration gains."
  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Asked about confidence in the $250 million-plus operating income growth target for FY '26. Snee responded, "For us, at this point, nothing has changed... nothing has changed in our outlook or from our perspective."
  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens: Asked about Foodservice margins and international mix. Snee said, "We do expect segment profit growth from Foodservice in the back half of the year," and confirmed a temporary issue in international margins was "fixed."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was pressing but constructive, focusing on the achievability of back-half growth, margin expansion, and recovery of Planters and Turkey businesses.
  • Management maintained a confident and consistent tone, using phrases such as "we feel confident in our second half" and "we are confident in our growth trajectory," with Smiley reiterating "we remain confident in our outlook for bottom line growth for each segment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained focused on execution but management's tone was slightly more assertive regarding the company's positioning for the second half.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company narrowed its net sales growth outlook from the previous 1%-3% range to 2%-3% for fiscal 2025, with adjusted EPS range slightly narrowed to $1.58-$1.68 from $1.58-$1.72.
  • The previous quarter emphasized challenges in Turkey supply and Planters recovery, while the current quarter's narrative centers on momentum in flagship brands, operational execution, and leadership transitions.
  • Analysts continued to question the achievability of the second-half ramp, but management showed increased confidence in segment performance and T&M benefits.
  • Key financial metrics, including gross margin and net sales, showed sequential improvement, and leadership transitions were explicitly outlined in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "anticipated higher commodity input costs" as a continued headwind, though partially offset by T&M savings.
  • Interest and investment income remains a drag, "not anticipated to be recouped."
  • Tariffs are expected to impact second-half results by $0.01 to $0.02 per share.
  • Tightening Turkey supply and evolving competitive dynamics were highlighted, though management expressed confidence in its value-added strategy.

Final Takeaway

Hormel Foods delivered solid second quarter results and reaffirmed its commitment to profitable, predictable growth, narrowing its fiscal 2025 outlook for net sales and EPS, and signaling strong momentum in core brands, operational transformation, and leadership continuity. The company anticipates robust second-half performance, driven by continued recovery and growth in Planters, turkey portfolio strength, and disciplined execution of its Transform and Modernize initiative, while remaining vigilant to input cost pressures and dynamic market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nordson outlines Q3 2025 sales guidance of $710M–$750M amid strategic portfolio actions and robust ATS demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nordson Corporation (NDSN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Sundaram Nagarajan opened by highlighting increasing order entry and backlog momentum, stating that "we started the second quarter with increasing momentum in order entry and backlog, enabling us to outperform the midpoint of our sales and earnings guidance." He noted strong performance in Advanced Technology system and parts sales, ongoing customer demand in semiconductor and selected electronics, as well as solid growth in nonwovens systems, medical fluid components, and precision agriculture. Nagarajan reported that the Atrion integration continues to "perform above our valuation model expectations."
  • He announced the agreement to divest select product lines in the Medical Contract Manufacturing business, explaining, "exiting these product lines will increase focus on higher value growth opportunities within the $800 million Medical and Fluid Solutions segment, namely within our growing portfolio of proprietary medical components."
  • Nagarajan emphasized operational excellence, sharing that "operational excellence during the quarter drove strong profit performance resulting in 32% overall EBITDA margins."
  • CFO Daniel Hopgood stated, "second quarter fiscal 2025 sales were $683 million up 5% from the prior year second quarter sales of $651 million." EBITDA adjusted for restructuring and integration costs was $217 million or 32% of sales, marking a 7% increase from the prior year. Hopgood added, "the impact of tariffs was not material to the company's operating financial performance in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Nordson expects third quarter fiscal 2025 sales to be in the range of $710 million to $750 million. Third quarter adjusted earnings are forecasted to be between $2.55 and $2.75 per diluted share.
  • Nagarajan described the guidance as "in line with the full year expectations we set at the beginning of our fiscal year and confirmed in our Q1 earnings call."
  • The company noted that "current tariff levels" are manageable and does not expect policies to have a material impact on results.

Financial Results

  • Hopgood reported sales of $683 million, a 5% increase from the prior year. Gross profit was $374 million, representing 55% of sales. EBITDA, adjusted for restructuring and integration costs, was $217 million or 32% of sales.
  • Net income totaled $112 million or $1.97 per share on a GAAP basis. Adjusted earnings per share were $2.42, a 3% increase from the prior year's $2.34.
  • Segment results included Industrial Precision Solution sales of $319 million (down 8%), Medical and Fluid Solution sales of $203 million (up 20%, with Atrion contributing $51 million), and Advanced Technology Solutions sales of $161 million (up 18%).
  • At quarter end, cash on hand was approximately $130 million and net debt was about $2.1 billion, resulting in a leverage ratio of 2.4 times. Free cash flow for the quarter was $103 million.
  • The company repurchased $85 million in shares and paid $44 million in dividends during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Michael Halloran, Baird: Asked about trends through the rest of the year and sustainability in ATS and Medical. Nagarajan explained that "ATS in general, pretty good feel pretty good about it going playing out through the rest of the year," while Medical is seeing "Atrion contributing to growth, very solid performance above our model expectations." He added that destocking in interventional products is lessening.
  • Halloran also inquired about the Contract Manufacturing divestiture. Hopgood stated, "this is roughly 4% of our year-to-date sales in the Medical segment" and estimated the move would be "roughly a 100 basis points accretive to our Medical segment margins going forward post-sale."
  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas: Asked about ATS volatility and order improvement. Nagarajan responded, "this is a business inherently lumpy...but overall...this is the part of the cycle where it is starting to grow," with strong order entry supporting the outlook. Hopgood noted that "most of the growth we're seeing right now is really with our Asian customers."
  • Saree Boroditsky, Jefferies: Asked about margin performance in ATS. Nagarajan said, "we have adjusted the cost structure such that, in a downturn, the margins would be lower than where it is today...but it's not going to be a place where we have significantly lower margin performance like seven years, five years ago."
  • Christopher Glynn, Oppenheimer: Probed on Atrion's upside. Nagarajan responded, "we're integrating nicely. We're certainly performing well commercially. They've got one new product out there that we're doing really well."
  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: Inquired about tariff exposure. Hopgood replied, "tariffs are very manageable. And as we stated, really had no material impact on our second quarter results."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on segment growth consistency, margin sustainability, and the impact of global tariffs, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone as they acknowledged improvements but sought specifics on volatility and risk.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strong order momentum and operational execution. Nagarajan repeatedly highlighted, "we feel really good about the trends we see" and "order entry and backlog are building and are supportive."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism regarding guidance and order recovery to a more assertive and confident stance regarding growth, especially in ATS and Medical.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance for the next quarter remains unchanged from Q1, with management reiterating the sales and earnings range previously set.
  • Q2 reported stronger order entry and backlog growth, particularly in ATS and Medical segments, compared to Q1’s focus on order acceleration and backlog increase.
  • Segment performance in Q2 showed improvement in ATS and Medical, with particular emphasis on Atrion’s contribution exceeding expectations, whereas Q1 highlighted operational resilience amid lower organic sales.
  • Analyst questions in Q2 focused more on the sustainability of growth trends and margin improvements versus Q1’s focus on recovery timing and segment realignment.
  • Management expressed higher confidence in segment outlooks and operational adaptability in Q2, compared to the more cautious approach in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing geopolitical and trade policy uncertainties, particularly regarding tariffs, were cited as a challenge. Nagarajan stated, "we remain agile in our action plans, knowing there is still plenty of market uncertainty due to tariffs."
  • Weaker customer demand in select industrial system sales persists, though sequential improvement was noted.
  • Management implemented targeted restructuring to adjust cost structure in weaker businesses, expecting $50 million in ongoing annual benefits by 2026.
  • The potential impact of tariffs on end-market demand remains a key risk, though current exposure is described as manageable.

Final Takeaway

Nordson’s management highlighted continued momentum in order entry and backlog, particularly in Advanced Technology and Medical segments, supporting the reaffirmed Q3 guidance range for sales and earnings. Strategic actions, including the divestiture of select Medical Contract Manufacturing product lines and strong operational execution, position the company for improved long-term growth and margin accretion. Management remains confident in navigating ongoing market and policy uncertainties, underpinned by a robust balance sheet and a focus on high-value proprietary products and operational agility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: IDC chops 2025 smartphone forecast amid economic uncertainty, including tariffs
data.description:
Research firm IDC cut its forecast for smartphone shipments this year, citing significant economic uncertainty stemming from a number of factors, including tariffs.

“Since April 2nd, the smartphone industry has faced a whirlwind of uncertainty,” IDC senior research director Nabila Popal said in a statement, referring to the Trump administration's “Liberation Day” tariff announcement.

“While current exemptions on smartphones have offered temporary relief, the looming possibility of broader tariffs presents a serious risk,” Popal added. “Recent signals from the US administration on potential tariffs hikes on smartphones manufactured outside the US further complicate long-term strategic planning for OEMs. Smartphone vendors — particularly those shipping to the US — must now navigate complex geopolitics alongside ongoing supply chain diversification efforts.”

IDC now expects worldwide smartphone shipments — which includes those made by Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and others — to rise just 0.6% year-over-year to 1.24B units, down from a prior view of 2.3% growth.

Popal said that tariff concerns on countries such as India, Vietnam and China could wind up posing “a serious downside risk to the current U.S. market outlook.”

Delving deeper, IDC said that the U.S. and China are largely responsible for the 0.6% growth, but China is expected to see 3% year-over-year growth, due largely to government subsidies. This is expected to largely benefit Android (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) devices, while Apple could see increased competition from companies like Huawei and other Chinese smartphone manufacturers.

Apple is expected to see a 1.9% year-over-year decline in iPhone shipments in 2025, IDC said, citing increased competition, an economic slowdown and the fact that Chinese government subsidies are capped at 6,000 yuan.

While Apple's iPhone shipments are expected to decline, the upcoming 618 shopping festivals in China, along with recent increases in trade-in discounts and the upcoming iPhone 17 release later this year could boost demand, IDC added.
